step_by_step_thinking: From the articles cited, it's clear that acromegaly specifically refers to the condition arising post-fusion of the epiphyses, meaning after childhood during adult years when growth stops. Specifically, one key passage states directly "[acromegaly] occurs...after the fusion of the epiphysis leading to large extremities and characteristic facies." Additionally, another reference clarifies that "Adults with acromegaly present with soft tissue overgrowth and skin thickening," implying no further bone growth since the epiphyses had fused prior to symptom development. Thus, we must conclude that option A regarding pre-closure of epiphyses does not apply - only option B accurately describes when acromegaly manifests chronologically relative to epiphysis status.
raw_response:
From the articles cited, it's clear that acromegaly specifically refers to the condition arising post-fusion of the epiphyses, meaning after childhood during adult years when growth stops. Specifically, one key passage states directly "[acromegaly] occurs...after the fusion of the epiphysis leading to large extremities and characteristic facies." Additionally, another reference clarifies that "Adults with acromegaly present with soft tissue overgrowth and skin thickening," implying no further bone growth since the epiphyses had fused prior to symptom development. Thus, we must conclude that option A regarding pre-closure of epiphyses does not apply - only option B accurately describes when acromegaly manifests chronologically relative to epiphysis status.
\boxed{B}
